consultant.executiveSummary: Oakwood Learning Studio projects $400,145 in Year 5 revenue with a 37.6% profit margin. Margins are on the thinner side, and the recommendations below will help you build a more compelling financial story.
consultant.biggestRisk: No major red flags detected, so continue refining assumptions as you gather real data.

recommendations.title: Plan for Tuition Collection & Discount Risk
recommendations.description: Private schools face collection risk from late payments, withdrawals, and financial aid shortfalls. With 88.6% of revenue from tuition, build a 5–10% bad debt reserve into your budget. Factor in tuition discount rates for merit/need-based aid and maintain clear enrollment contracts with payment terms.

recommendations.title: Stress-Test Your Enrollment Assumptions
recommendations.description: Model what happens if enrollment comes in 20% below plan. Understanding your downside scenario helps you prepare contingency plans.

recommendations.title: Document Your Growth Strategy
recommendations.description: Great projections are backed by a clear plan. Document your marketing strategy, enrollment pipeline, and community outreach, as this is the roadmap that turns your numbers into reality.

consultant.lenderReadinessExplanation: Rating capped at Needs Work pending evidence tagging on 22 of 22 assumptions. Below 30% evidence tagging, lenders in Lending Lab Cycle 1 bounced 6 of 7 packets with a 'show your work' response before opening any underwriting conversation. The model has not demonstrated assumption rigor to a lender-credible threshold. This model demonstrates strong financial fundamentals: a clear path to profit, controlled costs, a sustainable revenue mix, and adequate debt coverage. Your projections are grounded and achievable.

capTier.rationale: Below 30% evidence tagging, lenders in Lending Lab Cycle 1 bounced 6 of 7 packets with a 'show your work' response before opening any underwriting conversation. The model has not demonstrated assumption rigor to a lender-credible threshold.
capTier.source: Lending Lab Cycle 1 outcomes (Jan–Apr 2026, 18 packets, 3 lenders, 3 personas) — see ./lender-readiness-caps.calibration.md

cap.reason: Below 30% evidence tagging, lenders in Lending Lab Cycle 1 bounced 6 of 7 packets with a 'show your work' response before opening any underwriting conversation. The model has not demonstrated assumption rigor to a lender-credible threshold.

keyMetrics.name: Revenue per Student (Year 1)
keyMetrics.interpretation: Per-student revenue is moderate, so consider whether tuition or supplemental funding can increase.

keyMetrics.name: Cost per Student (Year 1)
keyMetrics.interpretation: Cost per student ($7,162) is approaching revenue per student ($8,282). Monitor expenses closely to maintain a sustainable margin.

keyMetrics.name: Staffing Cost (% of Revenue)
keyMetrics.interpretation: Staffing costs are well-controlled, giving you room for other priorities.

keyMetrics.name: Operating Cost (% of Revenue)
keyMetrics.interpretation: Operating costs are moderate, so watch rent escalation and service costs over the 5-year period.

keyMetrics.name: Profit margin (Year 1)
keyMetrics.interpretation: Year 1 shows a healthy surplus, a strong start for a new school.

keyMetrics.name: Profit margin (Year 5)
keyMetrics.interpretation: By Year 5 the model shows strong profit, and your school is on track for financial sustainability.

keyMetrics.name: 5-Year Revenue Growth
keyMetrics.interpretation: Strong projected revenue growth over the 5-year period.

keyMetrics.name: Capacity Utilization (Year 5)
keyMetrics.interpretation: Year 5 enrollment approaches facility capacity, an efficient use of space.

keyMetrics.name: Days Cash on Hand (Year 1)
keyMetrics.interpretation: 57 days of cash on hand is above the minimum but below the 90-day target. Build reserves to create a larger buffer.

keyMetrics.name: Cash Trough (Minimum Cash Month)
keyMetrics.interpretation: Your lowest cash point is Month 1 at $647. Lenders look at this to make sure you won't run out of money before tuition collections start. Consider building reserves.
keyMetrics.benchmark: Target: Positive cash balance every month

keyMetrics.name: Debt Service Coverage Ratio (Year 1)
keyMetrics.interpretation: DSCR is above 1.25x, and your operating income comfortably covers debt payments.

keyMetrics.name: Philanthropy (% of Revenue)
keyMetrics.interpretation: Philanthropy supplements earned revenue — a healthy mix.

keyMetrics.name: Operating Reserve (Year 5)
keyMetrics.interpretation: By Year 5, the school has built a healthy operating reserve of 3+ months, a strong foundation for long-term stability.

enrollmentGuidance: Year 1 to Year 2 projects 30% enrollment growth. Growth over 25% in a single year is uncommon and may require aggressive marketing or facility expansion.

Strong demand signal: 43 applications + waitlist entries cover 215% of Year 1 projected enrollment (20 seats). Your projections are backed by real demand, which is exactly the kind of evidence that makes a model credible.

topIssues.title: Let's extend your cash runway
topIssues.summary: Based on your projected revenue and expenses, cash currently lasts 3.1 months. Aiming for 18+ months gives you room to absorb late tuition payments or delayed public funding without scrambling.
topIssues.whyItMatters: An 18-month runway means a single late tuition cycle or delayed state disbursement isn't an emergency. Building the cushion now is far easier than rebuilding it after opening day.
topIssues.recommendedAction: Build a cash buffer through startup fundraising, a line of credit, or by reducing early expenses. Target at least 18 months of runway before opening.
topIssues.nextStep: Open Step 2: School Details and raise opening cash, or trim Year 1 expenses in Step 7, until projected cash runway clears 18 months.

topIssues.title: Let's back the enrollment ramp with evidence
topIssues.summary: Year 1 to Year 2 projects 30% enrollment growth. Adding documented demand (waitlists, signed letters, or a clear recruitment pipeline) makes this story land.
topIssues.whyItMatters: Enrollment is the engine of every revenue and staffing assumption downstream. The more evidence we can attach to each year's number, the more confident the rest of the model becomes.
topIssues.recommendedAction: Back every enrollment target with documented demand — signed letters of intent, waitlist depth, community survey results, or recruitment pipeline data. Growth over 25% per year typically requires an exceptional recruitment engine or facility expansion.
topIssues.nextStep: Open Step 4: Enrollment and either soften the steepest year to a 15-25% jump, or paste your waitlist count, signed letters of intent, and recruitment plan into the Story step so reviewers see the evidence.

topIssues.title: Let's tie new hires to enrollment milestones
topIssues.summary: In Year 2, staffing cost growth runs more than 10 points ahead of enrollment growth. Sequencing hires to match enrollment keeps the model resilient.
topIssues.whyItMatters: Hiring slightly behind enrollment, rather than ahead of it, keeps fixed costs aligned with the revenue actually arriving. It also gives you a clean answer to 'what triggers each new role'.
topIssues.recommendedAction: Align staffing additions to enrollment milestones. Hire when students are enrolled, not when you hope they will be. Use part-time or contract roles to bridge gaps.
topIssues.nextStep: Open Step 6: Staffing and tie each new role's start date to a specific enrollment milestone (e.g. 'add when Y2 enrollment hits 60'), or push the role into a later year.

healthSignals.label: Needs attention
healthSignals.explanation: The model reaches profit early and sustains strong margins through Year 5.
healthSignals.watchItem: Compare your real Year 1 enrollment against this projection as students enroll.
healthSignals.nextStep: Re-check this dimension after every enrollment or major staffing change in Steps 4–6.

healthSignals.label: Needs attention
healthSignals.explanation: Cash stays positive, but the buffer thins to $5,200 in Jul of Year 1 — less than one month of operating burn. A small revenue miss could push it below zero.
healthSignals.watchItem: Watch the trough month closely and line up a credit facility before it hits.
healthSignals.nextStep: Open Step 2: School Details and raise opening cash, or trim Step 7: Expenses, until the trough holds at least one month of burn.

healthSignals.label: Needs attention
healthSignals.explanation: The school burns more cash than it generates in 10 months. Opening cash absorbs the gap today, but tight timing leaves no margin for late tuition or a delayed disbursement.
healthSignals.watchItem: Track receivables and payroll dates against the trough month so a single late payment doesn't tip the cumulative balance negative.
healthSignals.nextStep: Open Step 5: Revenue and tighten billing cadence (or grow an enrollment-driven line), or trim Step 7: Expenses, until monthly net stays positive.

healthSignals.label: Needs attention
healthSignals.explanation: Staffing at 52% of revenue leaves room for facilities, programs, and reserves.
healthSignals.watchItem: As you add staff, ensure staffing stays below 60% of revenue.
healthSignals.nextStep: Re-check after every staffing change in Step 6 to make sure payroll stays under 60% of revenue.

healthSignals.label: Needs attention
healthSignals.explanation: Facility costs at 17% of revenue are moderate but could become a constraint as other costs grow.
healthSignals.watchItem: Ensure enrollment growth keeps pace with any rent increases.
healthSignals.nextStep: Open Step 7: Expenses and confirm your facility line and any rent escalation; trim it if enrollment growth is uncertain.

healthSignals.label: Needs attention
healthSignals.explanation: DSCR of 5.77x provides comfortable coverage of debt payments with room to spare.
healthSignals.watchItem: Maintain this ratio as you take on any additional debt.
healthSignals.nextStep: Re-check DSCR after any new debt is added in Step 5 to keep coverage at or above 1.25x.

healthSignals.label: Needs attention
healthSignals.explanation: Revenue is anchored to enrollment-driven income — the strongest foundation for a school model. A focused revenue stream is a strength when backed by dependable demand.
healthSignals.watchItem: Continue to pressure-test enrollment assumptions: recruitment pipeline, waitlist depth, retention rates, and collection reliability.
healthSignals.nextStep: Pressure-test your enrollment pipeline each cycle and re-check this signal after Step 4 updates.

healthSignals.label: Needs attention
healthSignals.explanation: 57 days of cash on hand is above the minimum but below the 90-day target. A large unexpected cost could create a cash crunch.
healthSignals.watchItem: Build toward 90+ days of cash on hand by controlling early-year spending and building reserves.
healthSignals.nextStep: Open Step 2: School Details and raise opening cash, or trim Step 7: Expenses, until days cash on hand clears 90.

healthSignals.label: Needs attention
healthSignals.explanation: 18.9 months of reserves by Year 5 — a strong buffer for unexpected costs.
healthSignals.watchItem: Continue growing reserves toward 6 months for best-in-class financial health.
healthSignals.nextStep: Keep building toward 6 months of reserves through controlled spending in Step 7.

criteria.name: Leader Compensation
criteria.detail: School leader draws a salary. This is a fundamental requirement for sustainable operations.

criteria.name: Philanthropy Dependency
criteria.threshold: ≤25% in every year, decreasing over time
criteria.detail: Fundraising is healthy and encouraged. But your school must be able to keep the lights on without it. At full enrollment, your budget should balance on earned revenue alone. Grants should fund growth and mission extras, not basic operations.

criteria.name: Personnel Cost Ratio
criteria.detail: Personnel costs exceed 60% of total expenses. This leaves too little room for facilities, curriculum, and contingency. A well-run school allocates no more than 60 cents of every dollar spent to people.

criteria.name: Facility Cost Ratio
criteria.detail: Facility costs are well within the target range, leaving healthy budget room for personnel and programming.

criteria.name: Debt Service Coverage
criteria.detail: DSCR of 5.77x provides strong debt service coverage with healthy cushion across all years.

criteria.name: Current Ratio
criteria.detail: No accounts payable or current debt reported, so the current ratio cannot be evaluated. If your school has any bills due within 12 months, enter them under Opening Balances.

criteria.name: Net Margin Trajectory
criteria.detail: Net margin trajectory is healthy, reaching break-even by Year 3 and growing to a sustainable level.

criteria.name: Minimum Enrollment
criteria.detail: Year 1 enrollment of 20 students meets the minimum viability threshold.

criteria.name: Revenue Per Pupil
criteria.detail: Revenue per pupil of $8,282 meets the minimum threshold for sustainable operations.

lendingLabAssessment.summary: 1 area needs attention before applying. Most founders need 2–3 iterations to get here. Adjust your model and the assessment updates automatically.

assumptionFlags.defaultPrompt: Year 1 enrollment fills only 44% of your building capacity. Is this a phased growth strategy, or could you open with more students?
assumptionFlags.nextStep: Open Step 4: Enrollment and either grow Year 1 to fill at least 50% of capacity, or note the phased-growth strategy in Step 1: Story.

assumptionFlags.currentValue: Tuition covers 144% of Year 1 total expenses
assumptionFlags.defaultPrompt: Sticker tuition plus registration and activity fees fund 144% of your Year 1 operating cost base — a strong signal that the school can stand on tuition alone before philanthropy, vouchers, or grants. Tell the lender how you'll defend pricing and convert that capacity into collected revenue.
assumptionFlags.nextStep: Open Step 5: Revenue to confirm your collection rate and any scholarship discount, then capture your pricing-defense story in Step 1: Story so the lender packet leads with this strength.

scenarios.name: Enrollment -10%
scenarios.description: All five enrollment years scaled down by 10% — the standard lender enrollment-miss sensitivity.

scenarios.name: Enrollment -20%
scenarios.description: All five enrollment years scaled down by 20% — the stretch downside lenders use to confirm the school can survive a soft-launch.

scenarios.name: Public Funding Delayed 3 Months (public sources only)
scenarios.description: Year-1 ESA, school-choice, and public-funding receipts reduced by 25% (≈ 3 months delayed) to simulate a slow first disbursement.

scenarios.name: Rent Shock +25%
scenarios.description: Occupancy & facility expense rows escalated by 25% — covers a lease renewal, market-rate reset, or unbudgeted CAM/utilities surprise.

scenarios.name: Founder Comp Normalized to Market
scenarios.description: Founder compensation re-cast at the market-rate benchmark (with benefits + payroll tax). The lender / board view of true operating cost.

narrative.headline: This model shows promise but has areas that should be strengthened before presenting to lenders.
narrative.summary: Oakwood Learning Studio projects $400,145 in Year 5 revenue with a 37.6% profit margin. Margins are on the thinner side, and the recommendations below will help you build a more compelling financial story. 0 of 9 health dimensions are healthy, while 9 need attention. Cash runway extends 3.1 months before additional funding would be needed.
keyRisks: No major red flags detected, so continue refining assumptions as you gather real data.

Let's extend your cash runway: Based on your projected revenue and expenses, cash currently lasts 3.1 months. Aiming for 18+ months gives you room to absorb late tuition payments or delayed public funding without scrambling.

Let's back the enrollment ramp with evidence: Year 1 to Year 2 projects 30% enrollment growth. Adding documented demand (waitlists, signed letters, or a clear recruitment pipeline) makes this story land.
Let's tie new hires to enrollment milestones: In Year 2, staffing cost growth runs more than 10 points ahead of enrollment growth. Sequencing hires to match enrollment keeps the model resilient.
Viability: The model reaches profit early and sustains strong margins through Year 5.
keyStrengths: Staffing Cost (% of Revenue): 52.0% — Staffing costs are well-controlled, giving you room for other priorities.
Profit margin (Year 1): 13.5% — Year 1 shows a healthy surplus, a strong start for a new school.
Profit margin (Year 5): 37.6% — By Year 5 the model shows strong profit, and your school is on track for financial sustainability.
5-Year Revenue Growth: 141.6% — Strong projected revenue growth over the 5-year period.
narrative.recommendedFocus: Continue refining the model and monitoring key metrics as the school develops.

sections.title: Cover Page
sections.narrative: Financial model prepared for Oakwood Learning Studio.

sections.title: Executive Summary
sections.narrative: Oakwood Learning Studio projects $400,145 in Year 5 revenue with a 37.6% profit margin. Margins are on the thinner side, and the recommendations below will help you build a more compelling financial story.

linkedMetrics.label: Biggest Risk
linkedMetrics.value: No major red flags detected, so continue refining assumptions as you gather real data.

sections.title: School Overview
sections.narrative: Oakwood Learning Studio is a Startup / Pre-Opening Microschool organized as a LLC (Single Member) with a Tuition-Based funding profile.

sections.title: Enrollment Plan
sections.narrative: Enrollment starts at 20 students in Year 1 and grows to 40 by Year 5 (100% growth).

sections.title: Revenue Model
sections.narrative: The revenue model is tuition-driven (88.6% of Year 1 revenue). Year 1 total revenue is projected at $166K, growing to $400K by Year 5.

rows.label: Residual reconciliation (collection rate, delinquency, scholarship treatment, other engine adjustments)

tables.title: Revenue Quality (Contracted vs. Projected vs. Donor / Policy Dependent)

rows.label: Hard revenue coverage of fixed + debt

sections.title: Staffing Plan
sections.narrative:
Staffing costs are $86K in Year 1, representing 52.0% of revenue. This is within the typical 50-60% range. Founder compensation is normalized to market rate for the lender / board view. The 5-year normalization adjustment increases fully-loaded staffing cost by +$67,895 (salary + benefits + payroll tax). Year-1 DSCR moves from 5.86x to 3.11x and cash runway moves from 3.1 to 1.8 months when normalized. DSCR, runway, and net income figures elsewhere in this packet reflect the normalized view; the founder dashboard reflects the as-planned view.

Suggested founder compensation steps up year over year as projected enrollment crosses NAIS / NACSA size bands (escalated by COLA from Y1). Source: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Occupational Employment & Wage Statistics, May 2023. Education Administrators, Kindergarten through Secondary (11-9032), national median annual wage..

tables.title: Suggested Founder Compensation Benchmark by Year
As-Planned vs Normalized: Net Income & DSCR

sections.title: Expense Summary
sections.narrative: Total expenses grow from $143K in Year 1 to $250K by Year 5.

sections.title: Capital & Debt
sections.narrative: The model includes 1 debt instrument totaling $20K.

sections.title: 5-Year Financial Projection
sections.narrative: The model reaches break-even in Year 1. Year 5 net income is projected at $150K (37.6% margin).

linkedMetrics.label: Cash Trough (Minimum Cash Month)
linkedMetrics.benchmark: Target: Positive cash balance every month

insights.label: Breakeven enrollment
insights.body: Breakeven enrollment is 18 students (11% above Year 1 enrollment).

insights.label: Accounting basis
insights.body: All projections are prepared on an accrual basis; the school currently keeps books on a undetermined basis.

sections.title: Prior-Year Actuals
sections.narrative: No prior-year data provided.

sections.title: Opening Balance Sheet
sections.narrative: Net position at model start: $15K.

sections.title: Facility & Key Performance Indicators
sections.narrative: Breakeven enrollment is 18 students. Year 1 projects 20 students, providing a 11.1% cushion.

sections.title: Cash Flow Analysis
sections.narrative: Year-end cash covers only 3.1 months of fixed costs. The school may need additional funding or cost adjustments. Opening balance: $15K total assets, $0 total liabilities, $15K net position. Lowest cash month across the 5-year forecast: Year 1 — Jul at $4K.

sections.title: Debt Service Coverage
sections.narrative: Year-1 debt service coverage is 3.11x on the normalized (lender-primary) view. Minimum: 1.15x; target: 1.25x. See the "As-Planned vs Normalized: Net Income & DSCR" table for the founder / board (as-planned) view alongside this normalized series.

sections.title: Stress Testing
sections.narrative: All 6 stress scenarios maintain positive Year 5 net income, indicating financial resilience.

sections.title: Lender Readiness
sections.narrative: Rating capped at Needs Work pending evidence tagging on 22 of 22 assumptions. Below 30% evidence tagging, lenders in Lending Lab Cycle 1 bounced 6 of 7 packets with a 'show your work' response before opening any underwriting conversation. The model has not demonstrated assumption rigor to a lender-credible threshold. This model demonstrates strong financial fundamentals: a clear path to profit, controlled costs, a sustainable revenue mix, and adequate debt coverage. Your projections are grounded and achievable.

sections.title: Key Risks
sections.narrative: 12 risk factors have been identified. Of these, 3 are rated critical and should be addressed prior to lender submission.

linkedMetrics.label: Let's back the enrollment ramp with evidence
Let's tie new hires to enrollment milestones

rows.label: Let's extend your cash runway
values: An 18-month runway means a single late tuition cycle or delayed state disbursement isn't an emergency. Building the cushion now is far easier than rebuilding it after opening day.

Build a cash buffer through startup fundraising, a line of credit, or by reducing early expenses. Target at least 18 months of runway before opening. Next step: Open Step 2: School Details and raise opening cash, or trim Year 1 expenses in Step 7, until projected cash runway clears 18 months.

rows.label: Let's back the enrollment ramp with evidence
values: Enrollment is the engine of every revenue and staffing assumption downstream. The more evidence we can attach to each year's number, the more confident the rest of the model becomes.

Back every enrollment target with documented demand — signed letters of intent, waitlist depth, community survey results, or recruitment pipeline data. Growth over 25% per year typically requires an exceptional recruitment engine or facility expansion. Next step: Open Step 4: Enrollment and either soften the steepest year to a 15-25% jump, or paste your waitlist count, signed letters of intent, and recruitment plan into the Story step so reviewers see the evidence.

rows.label: Let's tie new hires to enrollment milestones
values: Hiring slightly behind enrollment, rather than ahead of it, keeps fixed costs aligned with the revenue actually arriving. It also gives you a clean answer to 'what triggers each new role'.

Align staffing additions to enrollment milestones. Hire when students are enrolled, not when you hope they will be. Use part-time or contract roles to bridge gaps. Next step: Open Step 6: Staffing and tie each new role's start date to a specific enrollment milestone (e.g. 'add when Y2 enrollment hits 60'), or push the role into a later year.

rows.label: Viability
values: The model reaches profit early and sustains strong margins through Year 5.
Review revenue assumptions and cost structure to ensure operating margins support long-term sustainability.

rows.label: Liquidity
values: Cash stays positive, but the buffer thins to $5,200 in Jul of Year 1 — less than one month of operating burn. A small revenue miss could push it below zero.
Build operating reserves through fundraising, lines of credit, or phased expense deployment to maintain adequate cash position.

rows.label: Cash flow timing
values: The school burns more cash than it generates in 10 months. Opening cash absorbs the gap today, but tight timing leaves no margin for late tuition or a delayed disbursement.
Monitor this metric and develop a remediation plan if trends persist.

rows.label: Staffing burden
values: Staffing at 52% of revenue leaves room for facilities, programs, and reserves.
Evaluate staffing ratios against enrollment growth and consider phased hiring aligned to revenue milestones.

rows.label: Facility burden
values: Facility costs at 17% of revenue are moderate but could become a constraint as other costs grow.
Negotiate facility costs or explore co-location to keep occupancy below 15-20% of total revenue.

rows.label: Debt affordability
values: DSCR of 5.77x provides comfortable coverage of debt payments with room to spare.
Restructure debt terms, extend maturities, or increase operating income to improve Debt Service Coverage Ratio.

rows.label: Revenue concentration
values: Revenue is anchored to enrollment-driven income — the strongest foundation for a school model. A focused revenue stream is a strength when backed by dependable demand.
Strengthen enrollment pipeline evidence (waitlist, LOIs, retention data) and ensure revenue is anchored to demand-driven income rather than uncertain grants.

rows.label: Days cash
values: 57 days of cash on hand is above the minimum but below the 90-day target. A large unexpected cost could create a cash crunch.
Monitor this metric and develop a remediation plan if trends persist.

rows.label: Reserve strength
values: 18.9 months of reserves by Year 5 — a strong buffer for unexpected costs.
Establish a reserve fund policy targeting 3-6 months of operating expenses.

sections.title: Decision History
sections.narrative: No decisions have been tracked with an outcome yet. As the team marks saved scenarios as Pursued, Declined, or On hold, those outcomes (and any retrospective notes) will appear here so reviewers can see what actually happened versus what was modeled.

sections.title: Appendix: Key Assumptions
sections.narrative: Complete list of 21 assumptions used in this financial model, with each headline metric tied to its driving assumptions.

linkedAssumptions.label: — Assumption sources by headline metric —

riskMitigants.mitigant: Build a cash buffer through startup fundraising, a line of credit, or by reducing early expenses. Target at least 18 months of runway before opening. Next step: Open Step 2: School Details and raise opening cash, or trim Year 1 expenses in Step 7, until projected cash runway clears 18 months.
riskMitigants.whyItMatters: An 18-month runway means a single late tuition cycle or delayed state disbursement isn't an emergency. Building the cushion now is far easier than rebuilding it after opening day.

riskMitigants.risk: Let's back the enrollment ramp with evidence
riskMitigants.mitigant: Back every enrollment target with documented demand — signed letters of intent, waitlist depth, community survey results, or recruitment pipeline data. Growth over 25% per year typically requires an exceptional recruitment engine or facility expansion. Next step: Open Step 4: Enrollment and either soften the steepest year to a 15-25% jump, or paste your waitlist count, signed letters of intent, and recruitment plan into the Story step so reviewers see the evidence.
riskMitigants.whyItMatters: Enrollment is the engine of every revenue and staffing assumption downstream. The more evidence we can attach to each year's number, the more confident the rest of the model becomes.

riskMitigants.risk: Let's tie new hires to enrollment milestones
riskMitigants.mitigant: Align staffing additions to enrollment milestones. Hire when students are enrolled, not when you hope they will be. Use part-time or contract roles to bridge gaps. Next step: Open Step 6: Staffing and tie each new role's start date to a specific enrollment milestone (e.g. 'add when Y2 enrollment hits 60'), or push the role into a later year.
riskMitigants.whyItMatters: Hiring slightly behind enrollment, rather than ahead of it, keeps fixed costs aligned with the revenue actually arriving. It also gives you a clean answer to 'what triggers each new role'.

riskMitigants.mitigant: Review revenue assumptions and cost structure to ensure operating margins support long-term sustainability.
riskMitigants.whyItMatters: The model reaches profit early and sustains strong margins through Year 5.

riskMitigants.mitigant: Build operating reserves through fundraising, lines of credit, or phased expense deployment to maintain adequate cash position.
riskMitigants.whyItMatters: Cash stays positive, but the buffer thins to $5,200 in Jul of Year 1 — less than one month of operating burn. A small revenue miss could push it below zero.

riskMitigants.mitigant: Monitor this metric and develop a remediation plan if trends persist.
riskMitigants.whyItMatters: The school burns more cash than it generates in 10 months. Opening cash absorbs the gap today, but tight timing leaves no margin for late tuition or a delayed disbursement.

riskMitigants.mitigant: Evaluate staffing ratios against enrollment growth and consider phased hiring aligned to revenue milestones.
riskMitigants.whyItMatters: Staffing at 52% of revenue leaves room for facilities, programs, and reserves.

riskMitigants.mitigant: Negotiate facility costs or explore co-location to keep occupancy below 15-20% of total revenue.
riskMitigants.whyItMatters: Facility costs at 17% of revenue are moderate but could become a constraint as other costs grow.

riskMitigants.mitigant: Restructure debt terms, extend maturities, or increase operating income to improve Debt Service Coverage Ratio.
riskMitigants.whyItMatters: DSCR of 5.77x provides comfortable coverage of debt payments with room to spare.

riskMitigants.mitigant: Strengthen enrollment pipeline evidence (waitlist, LOIs, retention data) and ensure revenue is anchored to demand-driven income rather than uncertain grants.
riskMitigants.whyItMatters: Revenue is anchored to enrollment-driven income — the strongest foundation for a school model. A focused revenue stream is a strength when backed by dependable demand.

riskMitigants.mitigant: Monitor this metric and develop a remediation plan if trends persist.
riskMitigants.whyItMatters: 57 days of cash on hand is above the minimum but below the 90-day target. A large unexpected cost could create a cash crunch.

riskMitigants.mitigant: Establish a reserve fund policy targeting 3-6 months of operating expenses.
riskMitigants.whyItMatters: 18.9 months of reserves by Year 5 — a strong buffer for unexpected costs.

dscrSummary.trendDescription: Normalized (lender-primary) Y1 DSCR. See the “As-Planned vs Normalized: Net Income & DSCR” table for the founder / board (as-planned) view alongside the normalized series.

lenderReadiness.explanation: Rating capped at Needs Work pending evidence tagging on 22 of 22 assumptions. Below 30% evidence tagging, lenders in Lending Lab Cycle 1 bounced 6 of 7 packets with a 'show your work' response before opening any underwriting conversation. The model has not demonstrated assumption rigor to a lender-credible threshold. This model demonstrates strong financial fundamentals: a clear path to profit, controlled costs, a sustainable revenue mix, and adequate debt coverage. Your projections are grounded and achievable.

capTier.rationale: Below 30% evidence tagging, lenders in Lending Lab Cycle 1 bounced 6 of 7 packets with a 'show your work' response before opening any underwriting conversation. The model has not demonstrated assumption rigor to a lender-credible threshold.
capTier.source: Lending Lab Cycle 1 outcomes (Jan–Apr 2026, 18 packets, 3 lenders, 3 personas) — see ./lender-readiness-caps.calibration.md

cap.reason: Below 30% evidence tagging, lenders in Lending Lab Cycle 1 bounced 6 of 7 packets with a 'show your work' response before opening any underwriting conversation. The model has not demonstrated assumption rigor to a lender-credible threshold.

result.callout: Rating capped at Needs Work pending evidence tagging on 22 of 22 assumptions. Below 30% evidence tagging, lenders in Lending Lab Cycle 1 bounced 6 of 7 packets with a 'show your work' response before opening any underwriting conversation. The model has not demonstrated assumption rigor to a lender-credible threshold.

healthDimensionCap.callout: Health Dimensions ratings capped at at_risk pending evidence tagging on 22 of 22 assumptions. Below 25% evidence tagging, a healthy or watch-level signal cannot be trusted — the underlying inputs have not been anchored to evidence a lender or board can verify.

riskSeverityCap.callout: Risk Severity ratings raised to critical pending evidence tagging on 22 of 22 assumptions. Below 25% evidence tagging, a 'low' or 'medium' risk band is not credible — the inputs that would otherwise prove the risk is contained have not been anchored to evidence. The severity is floored at 'critical' so the gap surfaces, not the false comfort.

flag.defaultPrompt: Year 1 enrollment fills only 44% of your building capacity. Is this a phased growth strategy, or could you open with more students?
flag.nextStep: Open Step 4: Enrollment and either grow Year 1 to fill at least 50% of capacity, or note the phased-growth strategy in Step 1: Story.

flag.currentValue: Tuition covers 144% of Year 1 total expenses
flag.defaultPrompt: Sticker tuition plus registration and activity fees fund 144% of your Year 1 operating cost base — a strong signal that the school can stand on tuition alone before philanthropy, vouchers, or grants. Tell the lender how you'll defend pricing and convert that capacity into collected revenue.
flag.nextStep: Open Step 5: Revenue to confirm your collection rate and any scholarship discount, then capture your pricing-defense story in Step 1: Story so the lender packet leads with this strength.

scenarios.name: Enrollment -10%
scenarios.description: All five enrollment years scaled down by 10% — the standard lender enrollment-miss sensitivity.

scenarios.name: Enrollment -20%
scenarios.description: All five enrollment years scaled down by 20% — the stretch downside lenders use to confirm the school can survive a soft-launch.

scenarios.name: Public Funding Delayed 3 Months (public sources only)
scenarios.description: Year-1 ESA, school-choice, and public-funding receipts reduced by 25% (≈ 3 months delayed) to simulate a slow first disbursement.

scenarios.name: Rent Shock +25%
scenarios.description: Occupancy & facility expense rows escalated by 25% — covers a lease renewal, market-rate reset, or unbudgeted CAM/utilities surprise.

scenarios.name: Founder Comp Normalized to Market
scenarios.description: Founder compensation re-cast at the market-rate benchmark (with benefits + payroll tax). The lender / board view of true operating cost.

paragraphs: Oakwood Learning Studio is a microschool, based in AZ. Oakwood Learning Studio projects enrollment of 20 students in Year 1, growing to 40 by Year 5 against a stated capacity of 45 seats. Based on the canonical financial engine that powers this packet, the model reads as: Almost there - a few targeted edits will tighten the story. Rating capped at Needs Work pending evidence tagging on 22 of 22 assumptions. Below 30% evidence tagging, lenders in Lending Lab Cycle 1 bounced 6 of 7 packets with a 'show your work' response before opening any underwriting conversation. The model has not demonstrated assumption rigor to a lender-credible threshold. This model demonstrates strong financial fundamentals: a clear path to profit, controlled costs, a sustainable revenue mix, and adequate debt coverage. Your projections are grounded and achievable.

On the normalized base case (founder compensation marked to market), Year 1 debt service coverage is 3.11x. Operating cash runway extends 3.1 months from the open before additional capital would be required, with the tightest position at $34,397 of ending cash in Year 1. The model crosses operating break-even in Year 1, and Year 1 requires 17 students to cover fixed costs and debt service (38% of stated capacity).

Year 1 revenue mix is 86% contracted, 3% projected enrollment-driven, 0% policy-dependent, and 12% donor-dependent. The top 3 risks surfaced are: Let's extend your cash runway (critical). Mitigant: Build a cash buffer through startup fundraising, a line of credit, or by reducing early expenses. Target at least 18 months of runway before opening.; Let's back the enrollment ramp with evidence (critical). Mitigant: Back every enrollment target with documented demand - signed letters of intent, waitlist depth, community survey results, or recruitment pipeline data. Growth over 25% per year typically requires an exceptional recruitment engine or facility expansion.; Let's tie new hires to enrollment milestones (critical). Mitigant: Align staffing additions to enrollment milestones. Hire when students are enrolled, not when you hope they will be. Use part-time or contract roles to bridge gaps..

Founder compensation has been normalized to market for underwriting. Across the modeled period the founder is drawing $67,895 below a market salary for the role. On a planned-comp basis the Year 1 DSCR would read 5.86x; on the normalized basis it lands at 3.11x, which is the figure used throughout this packet. The lender-primary view in this packet underwrites the school at the market cost of running it, not the founder's voluntary discount.

Under the toughest stress in the lender battery (Enrollment -20%), minimum DSCR holds at -0.06x and minimum ending cash at $5,200. Strong Year 5 profit The full risk and recommendation set follows in the body of the packet.

Below 30% evidence tagging, lenders in Lending Lab Cycle 1 bounced 6 of 7 packets with a 'show your work' response before opening any underwriting conversation. The model has not demonstrated assumption rigor to a lender-credible threshold. This model demonstrates strong financial fundamentals: a clear path to profit, controlled costs, a sustainable revenue mix, and adequate debt coverage. Your projections are grounded and achievable.
bundle.biggestRisk: No major red flags detected, so continue refining assumptions as you gather real data.

topRisks.title: Let's extend your cash runway
topRisks.summary: Based on your projected revenue and expenses, cash currently lasts 3.1 months. Aiming for 18+ months gives you room to absorb late tuition payments or delayed public funding without scrambling.
topRisks.mitigant: Build a cash buffer through startup fundraising, a line of credit, or by reducing early expenses. Target at least 18 months of runway before opening.

topRisks.title: Let's back the enrollment ramp with evidence
topRisks.summary: Year 1 to Year 2 projects 30% enrollment growth. Adding documented demand (waitlists, signed letters, or a clear recruitment pipeline) makes this story land.
topRisks.mitigant: Back every enrollment target with documented demand — signed letters of intent, waitlist depth, community survey results, or recruitment pipeline data. Growth over 25% per year typically requires an exceptional recruitment engine or facility expansion.

topRisks.title: Let's tie new hires to enrollment milestones
topRisks.summary: In Year 2, staffing cost growth runs more than 10 points ahead of enrollment growth. Sequencing hires to match enrollment keeps the model resilient.
topRisks.mitigant: Align staffing additions to enrollment milestones. Hire when students are enrolled, not when you hope they will be. Use part-time or contract roles to bridge gaps.
